abstract: We address the problem of maintaining provenance sketches under updates in dynamic databases, focusing on incremental maintenance of compact provenance representations to support fast query execution and data skipping in relational databases with frequent data updates. Our approach, leveraging in-memory processing and coarse-grained sketch optimization, enables efficient maintenance of provenance sketches for multivariate query workloads with high update frequencies, evaluated on metrics such as maintenance cost and query answering performance. This incremental maintenance framework supports real-time data processing and streaming query optimization in data-intensive applications.

abstract: Scalable spatial topology joins are crucial for applications like geo-spatial interlinking and spatial databases, involving complex spatial objects such as polygons and requiring precise topological relation identification through minimum bounding rectangles (MBRs) and DE-9IM matrix computations. This work optimizes the process using precomputed raster representations to reduce costly DE-9IM computations, focusing on multivariate spatial analysis and spatial data indexing for improved throughput and efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate significant performance gains, particularly for high-complexity object pairs and large-scale spatial datasets.

Lossy time series compression for IoT and sensor data is addressed through autocorrelation-preserving line simplification, focusing on preserving statistical features such as autocorrelation and partial-autocorrelation functions (ACF/PACF) crucial for downstream analytics like multivariate forecasting. This approach enables efficient compression of univariate and multivariate time series data while maintaining forecasting accuracy, evaluated on compression ratios and forecasting benchmarks. By leveraging incremental aggregate maintenance and parallelization strategies, the method achieves improved compression ratios and preserves complex statistical properties.

Collaborative scoping tackles the challenge of schema linkability assessment in multi-source schema integration, focusing on identifying linkable tables and attributes in highly heterogeneous datasets with diverse schema designs and domains. This approach enables self-supervised and distributed assessment of schema quality, facilitating efficient linkage search in multivariate schema matching scenarios with irregularly-structured data. By streamlining schemas locally, collaborative scoping improves robustness in domain-specific and heterogeneous schema matching settings, evaluated through robustness benchmarks and linkage search efficiency metrics.

Schema linking for Text-to-SQL query generation is improved through in-depth analysis of large language model capabilities, focusing on multivariate database table and column identification from natural language requests in resource-constrained settings with limited supervised fine-tuning. The approach involves question decomposition, prediction refinement, and schema enrichment to provide a more representative schema, evaluated on Spider and BIRD benchmarks for schema linking accuracy and overall Text-to-SQL performance under few-shot inference constraints. This work targets high-quality schema linking for irregularly-structured database queries and SQL generation.

Scalability in blockchain systems is achieved through sharded Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) protocols with smart contract processing, but existing approaches face challenges with concurrent execution and dynamic access patterns. This work addresses these challenges in the context of multivariate transaction processing, including single-shard and cross-shard transactions, with a focus on low-latency and high-throughput performance metrics. The solution features a DAG-based coordination protocol, dynamic concurrency control, and nonblocking shard reconfiguration, enabling concurrent smart contract execution and achieving a 50x throughput improvement in distributed ledger technology with real-time consensus progress.

This research tackles workload memory prediction for batch execution of multiple queries in database management systems, focusing on predicting overall memory demand for a batch rather than individual query memory requirements. By formulating workload memory prediction as a distribution regression problem, LearnedWMP leverages query template representation, low-rank query embeddings, and histogram-based distribution regression to enhance memory demand estimation for multivariate query workloads with complex execution plans. Experimental results demonstrate significant reductions in memory estimation errors, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and achieving faster training and inference times for in-memory analytic queries.

abstract: Geospatial data collection and frequency estimation are optimized while ensuring robust user privacy through adaptive spatial granularity adjustments based on data density, utilizing local differential privacy and adaptive grids. This approach is evaluated on synthetic and real-world datasets with varying grid sizes and privacy budgets, demonstrating superior performance in balancing privacy and utility. The method enables precise frequency estimates in privacy-sensitive location-based services applications, such as longitudinal geospatial analysis and privacy-preserving mapping.

This work tackles query performance explanation in hybrid transactional and analytical processing (HTAP) systems, focusing on multivariate query optimization and cross-engine performance comparison, particularly for OLAP and OLTP engines. By leveraging large language models and retrieval-augmented generation, it provides clear explanations of performance differences, utilizing techniques such as tree-CNN classification and knowledge base construction for efficient retrieval of historical query executions and expert-curated insights. The approach enables real-time, interpretable query performance analysis in HTAP systems, with applications in database optimization and user support.

Integrating heterogeneous datasets from diverse sources with inconsistent values, such as synonyms and abbreviations, is a crucial step in data science pipelines, particularly in open data scenarios with limited metadata. This work addresses the challenge of combining datasets with approximate or fuzzy matches using an extension of Full Disjunction, an associative operator that preserves and combines available information, and evaluates its performance on data quality tasks with irregularly-sampled and multivariate data. The proposed fuzzy integration approach is shown to enhance accuracy without significant time overhead, leveraging data-driven techniques for approximate matching and join operations.

This work tackles the problem of diverse unionable tuple discovery in data lakes, focusing on identifying tuples that can contribute new information to a query table while avoiding redundancy. The approach addresses multivariate table similarity and tuple diversity in the context of large-scale data lakes with redundant and versioned tables, leveraging clustering-based algorithms and embedding models for efficient and effective discovery. Evaluations on real data lake benchmarks demonstrate significant improvements in terms of speed and diversification quality, outperforming existing baselines in unionable table search and tuple representation.

This work addresses in-database text classification for large-scale datasets, such as multivariate categorical data and irregularly-sampled text sequences, with a focus on incremental learning, unlearning, and global/local explainability. BornSQL, a SQL-based algorithm, is applied to a benchmark database of scientific publications with high-dimensional feature spaces, achieving linear training time and millisecond-scale inference. The approach enables cost-effective model serving, exploratory data analysis, and data privacy in database management systems.

Cohesive subgraph discovery in hypergraphs is tackled through efficient locality-based indexing, enabling online retrieval of dense subgraphs in complex networks with higher-order interactions. This approach facilitates informed decision-making in applications such as network analysis and community detection, particularly in domains with irregularly-structured relationships and multivariate interactions. By leveraging indexing for fast subgraph matching, our technique achieves efficient querying and traversal of large-scale hypergraphs, evaluated on real-world networks with heterogeneous relationships and structural diversity.

abstract: This work addresses the problem of scaling multivariate data analysis to large datasets that exceed memory capacity, focusing on in-memory dataframes and big data processing in data science applications, with an emphasis on lazy evaluation, just-in-time compilation, and runtime optimizations. The approach is evaluated on various backend engines, including Dask, Pandas On Spark, and DuckDB, using benchmarks such as performance metrics and scalability tests. By leveraging static analysis and lazy evaluation, the framework achieves significant performance benefits for large-scale data processing and streaming dataframes.

Community detection in massive dense graphs, such as similarity graphs in recommendation systems and natural language processing, is hindered by scalability issues and high memory costs due to explicit edge weight storage. This work addresses community partitioning with high modularity in high-volume applications, focusing on multilevel community detection and graph clustering, with an emphasis on efficient processing of large-scale implicit graphs. The approach is evaluated on real-world datasets with millions of nodes, demonstrating scalability and performance in dense graph analysis and large-scale analytics.

Hierarchical clustering of variable-length time series is hindered by expensive dissimilarity measures like Dynamic Time Warping and Time Warp Edit Distance, which impede scalability to large collections. DendroTime addresses this challenge in interactive, real-time clustering scenarios, leveraging progressive hierarchical clustering to incrementally compute pairwise dissimilarities and support flexible ordering strategies for multivariate time series data. This approach enables fast and interactive clustering of irregularly-sampled time series collections with quadratic runtime complexity reduction.

Optimizing main-memory hash joins for large-scale data workloads requires balancing CPU and memory efficiency, particularly in multivariate query processing and high-performance computing environments. The proposed CAMEL-HT hash table addresses this challenge by leveraging cache-aware and memory-efficient design principles to minimize cache miss rates and optimize data prefetching, resulting in improved execution times and memory efficiency. This approach enables scalable and efficient hash join operations for irregularly-sampled and high-volume relational datasets.
author_emails: author0066@example.com; author0067@example.com; author0068@example.com; author0069@example.com; author0029@example.com; author0070@example.com

abstract: Reliable data transformation in heterogeneous datasets is addressed through a framework leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) for retrieving trusted transformation functions, such as converting Unix timestamps to human-readable dates or extracting specific elements from complex data formats, with a focus on balancing function retrieval accuracy and validation costs. The approach utilizes conformal prediction techniques with abstention and size constraints to ensure retrieval coverage while controlling validation costs in multivariate data processing tasks. Evaluations demonstrate a trade-off between retrieval size and validation efficiency in data analysis pipelines, handling abstention scenarios and size constraints effectively in irregularly-sampled datasets.

Optimizing cache management for LSM-tree key-value stores with complex read patterns and dynamic access patterns is crucial for high performance. AdCache tackles this challenge in the context of multivariate workload characteristics, leveraging reinforcement learning to adapt cache partitioning and admission policies for both point lookups and range scans, with a focus on memory efficiency and cache hit rate optimization. By selectively caching frequent keys and beneficial scan portions, AdCache achieves improved performance metrics, including reduced SST reads and higher cache hit rates, in RocksDB deployments with irregular access patterns.

Optimizing slow-executing complex SQL queries is crucial for enterprise database performance, particularly in multivariate query workloads with high selectivity and irregular query patterns. Leveraging large language models (LLMs) and ensemble prompting techniques, our approach achieves significant runtime speedups, outperforming state-of-the-art rewriters and native optimizers on standard benchmarks like TPC-DS, with improvements in query rewriting efficiency and semantic correctness. Our LLM-infused system demonstrates substantial performance gains, with geometric mean runtime speedups of 13.2 on PostgreSQL, in real-time database environments with streaming data and concurrent query execution.

This work tackles multivariate time series analysis and similarity querying on complex temporal phenomena, such as longitudinal medical histories, by introducing convergence queries that model increasing or decreasing similarity over time based on rich time semantics and interleaved time-gap semantics. The approach focuses on scalable processing of high-dimensional time series compounds with alignment complexity, using a distributed execution pipeline and query optimization strategies that minimize data partition access. Evaluation on terabyte-scale datasets demonstrates significant speedup and accuracy improvements for convergence queries, outperforming kNN-based solutions in terms of accuracy and system throughput.

Efficiently querying large-scale databases with multivariate data is increasingly challenging as data volumes grow, prompting the need for optimized indexing techniques in log-structured merge-tree (LSM-tree) systems. This work provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating learned indexes in LSM-tree systems, considering factors such as index types, boundary positions, and granularity, with a focus on lookup performance and retraining overhead. By analyzing eight existing learned indexes and their theoretical costs, we offer practical guidelines for selecting and tuning learned indexes in real-time database systems with irregularly-sampled data and streaming workloads.

Fast landmark reconfiguration is crucial for maintaining efficient querying of weighted digraph properties, such as distances and constrained shortest paths, in large-scale graph mining applications with tens of millions of arcs. This work focuses on dynamic graph indexing, specifically updating landmark sets in highway cover labeling (HCL) indexes, to enable near real-time queries and low space overhead, even in fully dynamic settings with frequent graph topology updates. The proposed algorithms achieve speedups of several orders of magnitude over full reprocessing, enabling fast and efficient landmark updates for massive graphs with irregular topology changes.

This work addresses fair center clustering in sliding windows over multivariate data with categorical attributes, focusing on minimizing maximum distance in general metrics while ensuring category-based representation. The proposed streaming algorithm operates under strict space and time constraints, providing near-optimal solutions for real-time clustering and streaming anomaly detection in irregularly-sampled time series. It achieves efficient clustering with category-aware constraints, enabling applications in fair data summarization and real-time decision-making.

Optimizing user-defined function (UDF) queries in SQL data engines is crucial for complex in-database computations, particularly for applications involving multivariate data processing and streaming data integration. This work focuses on reducing execution overhead in UDF queries through techniques like operator fusion and JIT compilation, with a goal of improving performance in relational databases with heterogeneous data types, including scalar, aggregate, and table functions. By enhancing query optimization for UDFs, this approach achieves significant speedups, outperforming current research and commercial systems in SQL database engines.

Optimizing analytical database systems with automated discovery of data dependencies beyond primary and foreign keys enables substantial performance gains for multivariate query workloads. By efficiently identifying and validating unique columns and order dependencies, our system achieves high geometric mean speedups, such as 35% for TPC-DS and 29% for JOB benchmarks, with improved query latencies and low dependency discovery overhead. This automated approach integrates data dependency-based query optimization into database systems, leveraging data dependencies for real-time query plan optimization without manual declaration or SQL rewrites.

Improving model fairness in critical domains like healthcare and finance requires careful selection of additional training data to mitigate unfairness while preserving accuracy. This work addresses the task of selective data expansion for multivariate datasets, focusing on fairness-aware model performance and leveraging techniques like influence functions and multi-armed bandits to identify valuable data points. The approach is evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating significant reductions in model unfairness with minimal impact on accuracy, measured by metrics such as accuracy reduction and fairness improvement.

Discovering partial inclusion dependencies (pINDs) in large relational datasets is crucial for data integration, query optimization, and integrity checking, particularly in the presence of data inaccuracies. This work focuses on scalable partial inclusion dependency discovery in multivariate relational data, leveraging techniques such as multi-column sort-merge joins, aggressive parallelization, and global sortation. The approach enables efficient discovery of both exact and partial INDs, with evaluations on real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrating significant performance improvements over existing algorithms like BINDER and SPIDER.

Accelerating graph construction for maximum inner product search (MIPS) in high-dimensional vector spaces is crucial for applications with large datasets, such as similarity search and recommender systems, where proximity graph construction is a computational bottleneck. Our approach optimizes the graph construction step of graph-based MIPS methods, like ip-NSW, by leveraging upper bounds of inner products to skip unnecessary computations, ensuring identical graph structures and maintaining search accuracy for multivariate data with high-dimensional feature spaces. This optimization technique enables efficient construction of proximity graphs for real-time similarity search and information retrieval applications.

abstract: Discovering frequent structures in conceptual models, particularly in multivariate graph-structured data, is a challenging task that requires efficient pattern mining techniques, such as frequent subgraph mining, to identify good or bad modeling practices. The proposed approach focuses on semantic graph mining and heuristic tasks in the context of conceptual modeling, with an emphasis on performance-oriented and interpretable solutions. It is evaluated on conceptual modeling benchmarks, comparing its performance to established state-of-the-art methods in terms of pattern discovery and recurring phenomenon representation.
author_emails: author0114@example.com; author0115@example.com; author0116@example.com; author0117@example.com; author0118@example.com; author0119@example.com; author0120@example.com; author0121@example.com

abstract: Optimizing big data engine performance, particularly for compute-intensive workloads, requires careful evaluation of native execution engines, such as Velox and DataFusion, in Apache Spark environments with SIMD instructions and vectorized execution. To address performance bottlenecks, we investigate the overhead of vector materialization and propose a cost model driven operator placement strategy, achieving up to 2.1× speedup, and explore hardware acceleration opportunities using GPU and FPGA for tasks like Apache ORC table scan, yielding up to 1.8× performance gains. Our work provides insights into adaptive scheduling and hardware acceleration for big data processing, including multivariate performance analysis and irregularly-sampled data processing.

Scalable streaming join placement and parallelization is crucial for minimizing latency in geo-distributed environments, particularly for latency-sensitive applications like environmental monitoring and anomaly detection that require efficient regional stream joins near data sources. In resource-constrained edge computing settings, optimizing operator replication and placement is essential to prevent node overload and mitigate network congestion, with evaluation metrics including latency reduction, throughput increase, and re-optimization times. Nova addresses the Operator Placement and Replication problem through a convex optimization approach, enabling linear scalability and efficient adaptation to topological changes in heterogeneous networks with real-time data processing and streaming anomaly detection.

This study evaluates the performance and resource efficiency of multi-tenancy strategies in PostgreSQL, specifically schema-per-tenant, database-per-tenant, and container-per-tenant models, using TPC-C and TPC-H benchmarks in a dockerized microservice architecture. The results highlight trade-offs among strict isolation, performance, and resource efficiency in cloud-native systems, with container-per-tenant approaches exceling in CPU-intensive transactional workloads like TPC-C, but struggling with memory-intensive analytical workloads like TPC-H. The evaluation focuses on resource sharing, isolation, and dockerized deployments orchestrated by Kubernetes.

Temporal graph analysis is tackled through efficient enumeration of all temporal k-cores within a given query time range, leveraging concepts like core times to achieve optimal algorithmic running time bounded by result size. This approach enables fast computation of temporal k-cores in large-scale temporal graphs with improved scalability. The method demonstrates significant efficiency gains over existing solutions in multivariate graph analysis, graph decomposition, and temporal network processing.
author_emails: author0131@example.com; author0132@example.com; author0133@example.com; author0134@example.com; author0135@example.com; author0136@example.com

abstract: Reliable text-to-SQL generation is crucial for preventing costly errors from incorrect queries, particularly in multivariate database querying and semantic ambiguity resolution. This work focuses on ensuring query reliability in natural language database interfaces, using uncertainty-aware pipelines and conformal prediction frameworks to provide formal statistical correctness guarantees, evaluated on benchmarks like Spider and BIRD with metrics including execution accuracy and human-in-the-loop review. By integrating execution entropy measures, the approach enables trustworthy database querying with real-time reliability guarantees and human oversight for low-confidence queries.

Knowledge graph validation is a crucial task for ensuring factual accuracy in applications that rely on structured knowledge, such as entity disambiguation and relationship extraction, particularly in domains with high-stakes decision-making like healthcare and finance. This work focuses on evaluating large language models for fact validation across internal knowledge, external evidence via retrieval-augmented generation, and aggregated knowledge using multi-model consensus strategies, with benchmarks on diverse real-world knowledge graphs and a retrieval-augmented generation dataset. The experimental results highlight the challenges of achieving stable and reliable performance in knowledge graph fact validation, emphasizing the need for systematic evaluation and progress on this task.

Solving complex querying challenges, such as handling NULL-values and SQL injection, in relational databases is achieved through a functional data model and query language, enabling expressive and type-safe querying capabilities for multivariate data and streaming updates. This approach integrates smoothly into existing programming languages, facilitating holistic optimization between compilers and databases for real-time data processing and irregularly-sampled data streams. By unifying query languages and programming languages, it enables advanced data modeling and querying for heterogeneous data sources.

Real-time co-movement mining in streaming trajectory data enables applications like congestion control and movement prediction, but existing methods often suffer from redundant sub-trajectory comparisons. This work tackles real-time co-movement mining in distributed environments, leveraging snapshot-based clustering, dynamic partitioning, and density-based clustering, with a focus on streaming scenarios, trajectory data, and computational latency. The approach achieves efficient co-movement pattern discovery, improving throughput and reducing latency, with applications in multivariate trajectory analysis, parallel clustering, and distributed DBSCAN.
author_emails: author0142@example.com; author0143@example.com; author0144@example.com; author0145@example.com; author0146@example.com; author0147@example.com; author0148@example.com; author0149@example.com; author0150@example.com

abstract: Automatic schema discovery for property graphs is crucial for efficient querying, integration, and exploration in domains like knowledge graphs and social networks, where schema-free data poses significant challenges. This work tackles incremental schema discovery for multivariate, interconnected data, focusing on latent node and edge type identification, property datatype inference, and constraint detection, with an emphasis on scalability and real-time data ingestion. By leveraging locality-sensitive hashing and clustering techniques, the approach achieves high accuracy and efficiency, outperforming existing solutions in terms of node and edge type accuracy and execution speed.
author_emails: author0151@example.com; author0152@example.com; author0153@example.com; author0154@example.com; author0155@example.com; author0156@example.com

abstract: Scalable web data acquisition for journalistic fact-checking and social or economic research requires efficient crawling of high-quality statistics datasets from large websites with millions of webpages. Our approach targets retrieval of specific resource types, such as statistics datasets, using a focused crawling algorithm that optimizes for efficient and scalable data collection, with evaluation on metrics like crawl coverage and target retrieval fractions. By leveraging reinforcement learning techniques, specifically sleeping bandits, our crawler achieves high efficiency in retrieving targets while minimizing webpage crawls.

Real-time multimodal stream processing is achieved through a system that integrates multilingual language models as first-class operators, enabling query processing across multiple modalities such as text, image, and audio streams. This approach requires optimizations for low-latency and high-throughput processing, including logical, physical, and semantic query transformations that balance model load and accuracy in streaming anomaly detection and multivariate forecasting applications. By leveraging such optimizations, significant performance improvements can be made, as demonstrated by the Samsara prototype, which showcases multimodal query processing and scalable stream processing capabilities.
author_emails: author0160@example.com; author0161@example.com; author0162@example.com; author0163@example.com; author0164@example.com; author0165@example.com

abstract: Optimizing query execution in data-intensive environments requires understanding the performance drivers of Learned Query Optimizers (LQOs) in multivariate query workloads. A systematic evaluation framework is applied to compare traditional query optimizers with LQOs, considering dimensions such as performance, robustness, and generalization, with a focus on internal decision-making and learning procedures. This analysis reveals critical dependencies on training strategies, model design, and workload characteristics, shedding light on LQO performance in complex query optimization scenarios.

Training large recommendation models in low-resource scenarios requires efficient storage of massive embedding tables, particularly when offloading to external storage like solid-state drives, which are challenged by random data accesses during training. RecDB addresses this problem by leveraging the Log-Structured Merge-Tree to optimize writes and incorporating techniques like request preprocessing and compaction scheduling to improve read performance and reduce storage waste in multivariate embedding tables. This approach enables cost-effective storage and achieves significant end-to-end training speedup in low-resource environments with irregularly-sampled data and high resource contention.

Real-time spatiotemporal stream processing of mobility trajectory data from Internet-of-Things sensors is addressed, focusing on edge-to-cloud settings with high-volume IoT workloads and low-latency requirements. The system is evaluated on real-time mobility queries, such as brake system monitoring and adaptive speed-limit suggestions, using a streaming workload of 20k events/s on edge devices like Raspberry Pi 5. This enables real-time analytics and decision-making on irregularly-sampled time series data from moving objects, with sustained near real-time latencies and support for spatiotemporal operations.
author_emails: author0174@example.com; author0175@example.com; author0176@example.com; author0177@example.com; author0178@example.com; author0179@example.com; author0180@example.com; author0181@example.com; author0182@example.com

abstract: Data summarization for large, complex datasets with numerous underlying clusters is addressed through a clustering approach that reduces redundancies in centroid-based summaries, particularly in multivariate data settings with high-dimensional feature spaces. The proposed method, building upon centroid-based clustering and leveraging techniques from representation learning, yields more succinct yet accurate summaries, as evaluated through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets with metrics assessing summary quality and size. This work focuses on high-dimensional data summarization, centroid-based clustering, and representation learning, with applications to topics like deep clustering and multivariate data analysis.

Entity resolution across heterogeneous data sources is addressed through the construction of a model repository, enabling efficient search and integration for multi-source entity resolution tasks, particularly in scenarios with limited labeled data and scalability concerns. The approach leverages feature distribution analysis for clustering similar entity resolution tasks, facilitating moderate labeling efforts and effective model reuse. Experimental evaluations on multi-source datasets demonstrate comparable or improved performance to active learning, transfer learning, and supervised transformer-based methods, with applications in data integration and continuous learning from irregularly-sampled data sources.

Streaming graph processing for real-time graph analytics requires efficient handling of edge updates and graph computations, particularly in domains with high-volume, varied batch sizes, such as multivariate graph updates and dynamic network analysis. DCSR addresses this challenge by introducing a leaf-oriented locking mechanism, enabling parallel updates with low conflicts and supporting fast graph updates, with applications in graph computation phase and edge insertion. This approach achieves high update throughput, such as million-scale updates per second, and outperforms existing dynamic data structures like PPCSR, CPMA, and PaC-tree, in terms of update performance and speedup.

This work addresses fairness control and model performance maintenance under data drift in real-world scenarios, focusing on multivariate fairness concerns and distribution shifts in datasets. The approach efficiently manages fairness and accuracy by identifying harmful data regions using cluster-based algorithms, applicable to various data modes, including tabular and irregularly-sampled data, with evaluation on real-world and synthetic datasets. It achieves efficiency through selective retraining or fine-tuning, optimized for streaming data and real-time deployment with metrics such as accuracy-fairness balance and data utilization.

Efficient information access over large-scale vector databases is achieved through adaptive query-aware hybrid search, combining vector and structured retrieval for multivariate data in AI-based applications. This approach leverages query history to inform entry point selection in multi-layer proximity graphs, enhancing convergence and accuracy in approximate nearest neighbour search. Experimental evaluations on diverse datasets demonstrate improvements in search efficiency and accuracy, with applications in high-dimensional data indexing and similarity search.

Managing complex, interconnected data with rich metadata and contextual information is a critical challenge in domains like knowledge graph construction and semantic data integration, where querying metadata and reification are essential. This work addresses the limitations of traditional property graph databases through the implementation and evaluation of Meta-Property Graphs (MPGs) in a relational database engine extension, leveraging relational algebra extensions and high-level query optimizations for metadata-aware and reification-heavy workloads. The approach is validated through a custom benchmark with a data generator and query suite, demonstrating its effectiveness on metadata-intensive workloads in the AvantGraph database system, with applications in multivariate graph querying and semantic data processing.

Optimizing distance comparison operations is crucial for efficient approximate nearest neighbor search in high-dimensional vector databases, particularly in applications with large-scale multivariate data and stringent real-time constraints. This work surveys and experimentally evaluates mainstream optimization techniques, including projection pruning and quantization, across diverse index structures and datasets with varying dimensionality and scale, focusing on time-accuracy trade-offs and preprocessing overhead. The evaluation provides performance characterizations for method selection in scenarios with irregularly-sampled high-dimensional data and streaming similarity search.

Efficient retrieval in large-scale recommendation systems is achieved through hierarchical indexing, enabling reduced retrieval costs while preserving exactness in multivariate search spaces with billions of users. This approach is particularly useful for real-time advertisement recommendations on social media platforms, such as Facebook and Instagram, where streaming data and high-performance computing resources are involved. By leveraging cross-attention and residual quantization, our method improves inference performance on irregularly-sampled user interaction data, outperforming strong baselines on internal and public datasets.

abstract: Optimizing in-memory database management systems for large data volumes, this work focuses on dynamic memory allocation of Compute Express Link (CXL) memory pools, particularly in enterprise settings with SAP HANA, to improve scalability and reduce total cost of ownership through efficient capacity sharing and minimized over-provisioning. The study evaluates CXL's impact on IMDBMS performance, considering latency and bandwidth constraints, and highlights use cases such as compute and data node disaggregation and dynamic assignment of intermediate query results in cloud environments. By analyzing CXL memory pools, the research demonstrates improved flexibility of server architecture and reduced costs in scalable, disaggregated memory architectures.
author_emails: author0225@example.com; author0226@example.com; author0227@example.com; author0228@example.com; author0229@example.com; author0101@example.com; author0230@example.com; author0231@example.com; author0232@example.com; author0233@example.com

abstract: Extracting key details from sustainability objectives in online reports is a crucial task for combating greenwashing, involving multivariate text analysis and sequence labeling of unstructured data in the sustainability domain. This work tackles the challenge of automatic detail extraction from irregularly-structured sustainability reports using weak supervision, with a focus on token-level labeling and fine-tuning for improved accuracy and real-world deployment. The approach is evaluated on sustainability objective extraction benchmarks, demonstrating significant impact in business applications with GoalSpotter integration.

Automating data collection for empirical research is tackled through a system that combines natural language query input with active learning to generate tabular datasets and prediction models. This approach handles unstructured data science needs, such as multivariate data extraction and predictive modeling, by leveraging large language models to prioritize costly function calls. The system enables efficient data science workflow automation, facilitating applications in data-intensive research domains like irregularly-sampled time series analysis and streaming data integration.

Vessel trajectory imputation is crucial for accurate maritime traffic analysis and real-time situational awareness, particularly in applications such as route planning, transportation logistics, and environmental monitoring. This work addresses the challenge of large gaps in vessel trajectories from Automatic Identification System (AIS) data, which can degrade data quality, by leveraging historical AIS data and motion patterns to inform imputation. The proposed framework, HABIT, enables efficient and accurate imputation of maritime trajectories, considering vessel characteristics and motion patterns, such as smooth turns and maneuvering near ports, in multivariate time series data with irregular sampling and coverage limitations.
author_emails: author0241@example.com; author0242@example.com; author0243@example.com; author0244@example.com; author0245@example.com; author0246@example.com; author0247@example.com

abstract: Massive-scale equi- and geospatial joins in hyperscale analytical data warehouses are optimized through dynamic prefiltering, which reduces I/O, data shuffling, and CPU-intensive join processing for trillions of rows. This approach is particularly effective for complex workloads such as geospatial analytics, petabyte-scale data, and Compute Adjacent Storage Architecture (CASA), leveraging techniques like probabilistic Bloom filters, spatial joins, and quadtree structures for adaptive spatial pruning. By integrating prefiltering with query scheduling and execution, dynamic prefiltering achieves significant performance gains, including 30x acceleration and 98% latency reduction for selective geospatial joins.

Improving model accuracy for hyperscale datasets in modern data warehouses requires scalable ensemble methods, such as multivariate forecasting and heterogeneous ensemble construction, with support for recursive composition and weighted resampling. This work focuses on in-database machine learning for streaming data warehouses, using techniques like gradient boosting and stacking to enhance price prediction accuracy, with evaluation on metrics like error reduction and code complexity. The approach enables SQL-native users to define sophisticated ensemble pipelines, including bagging and boosting, using a declarative framework with CREATE MLMODEL statements.

Schema matching for heterogeneous data sources is addressed through a hybrid approach that leverages contextual and structural similarities in multivariate schema pairs, utilizing scarce domain-specific data and dynamic prompts for enhanced semantic reasoning. This framework is applied to real-life datasets for data migration and integration, demonstrating improved performance over existing methods in terms of interoperability and alignment accuracy. The solution combines fine-tuned transformer models with large language models, enabling more accurate and scalable schema matching for enterprise tasks.
